title: 10 Hidar — St. Matra the Martyr & The Assembly
saints: St. Gideon the Judge and Prophet
narrative:
The tenth day of Hidar brings the monthly Matra into the apostolic assembly alongside two figures whose vesting was sudden, specific, and entirely unambiguous in its divine origin. St. Matra, returning monthly as the calendar's representative of unnamed faithful witness, provides the apostolic month with its necessary reminder that the vesting of the ordinary is as real as the vesting of the extraordinary — that the commission distributed in Hidar is not reserved for those who receive angelic visitations or military commissions, but is given in the quiet fact of the martyr's refusal, repeated across every month of the year until its repetition has become itself a form of the apostolic proclamation. Matra's monthly return in Hidar is the testimony that the apostolic vesting is not a single event but a continuous state — the permanent commissioning of the baptized into the witness that their baptism conferred.

St. Gideon the Judge is the Old Testament figure of the reluctant apostolic vesting par excellence — the man threshing wheat in a winepress to hide it from the Midianites when the angel of the Lord appeared and said, 'The Lord is with you, mighty warrior.' The greeting is addressed not to the man Gideon demonstrably is — hiding, frightened, performing the task of the weak rather than the strong — but to the man the divine commission will make him. The angel of the Lord vests Gideon with the title before he has earned it, because the apostolic vesting is always proleptic: it names what the person will become under the commission's formation, not what they currently are by their own assessment. Gideon's three hundred, the divided army, the torches hidden in jars — his victory is won through instruments of deliberate inadequacy, because the apostolic vesting is specifically designed to ensure that the glory returns to the One who sent, rather than to the one who was sent.

St. Theoctistus the Martyr — whose witness belongs to the great company of Palestinian and Near Eastern martyrs whose blood formed the deep subsoil of the apostolic Church — stands on the tenth day of Hidar as the testimony of the vesting that completes itself in death. The martyr who has been vested with the apostolic commission and who has carried it faithfully until the commission is completed by execution has not been abandoned by the one who vested him; he has fulfilled the commission's terms. In the apostolic month of Hidar, Theoctistus reminds the community that the apostolic vesting is not a guarantee of safety but a guarantee of significance — that every act of faithfulness under the commission's terms, whether or not it is witnessed or preserved in historical memory, counts fully in the divine accounting.

Matra, Gideon, and Theoctistus together on the tenth day compose the apostolic vesting in its recurring, reluctant, and completing expressions. Matra is vested monthly, perpetually, in the act of simple martyric faithfulness. Gideon is vested with a title he has not yet earned, in the winepress of his hiding, by an angel who addresses the warrior he does not yet know he is. Theoctistus is vested to completion — his commission fulfilled and sealed. The community in the tenth day of Hidar receives all three as models of the apostolic life in its full temporal range: the perpetual, the inaugural, and the final.
